Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Geometric Diagnostics for Efficient Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging curvature-aware geometric diagnostics (e.g., Hessian conditioning, gradient covariance decay) as deterministic proxies for precision requirements, we can design a training-free precision routing framework that optimizes mixed-precision inference while maintaining numerical stability and computational efficiency.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision methods often rely on heuristic or training-based approaches to determine precision levels, which can be computationally expensive and lack theoretical guarantees. Recent work on curvature diagnostics (e.g., Hessian-based methods) has shown promise in understanding optimization landscapes but has not been applied to precision routing. Our proposal distinguishes itself by formalizing curvature-aware diagnostics as deterministic proxies for precision requirements, enabling a training-free, theoretically grounded approach to mixed-precision inference.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference has emerged as a key strategy for optimizing deep learning models, but existing methods often rely on heuristic or training-based approaches that lack theoretical guarantees. We propose a novel framework, Curvature-Aware Precision Routing, which leverages curvature-aware geometric diagnostics (e.g., Hessian conditioning, gradient covariance decay) as deterministic proxies for precision requirements. By formalizing these diagnostics into a training-free precision routing protocol, our approach optimizes mixed-precision inference while maintaining numerical stability and computational efficiency. We validate our framework through rigorous empirical evaluation, demonstrating significant improvements in inference speed and resource efficiency without compromising model accuracy. Our results highlight the potential of curvature-aware diagnostics to bridge the gap between theoretical optimization theory and practical deployment constraints.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Validate curvature diagnostics (Hessian conditioning, gradient covariance decay) as proxies for precision requirements on standard benchmarks (e.g., ResNet, Transformer). Metrics: Precision reduction accuracy, inference speedup.
Experiment 2: Compare our training-free precision routing framework against heuristic and training-based baselines. Metrics: Inference speed, resource efficiency, model accuracy.
Experiment 3: Analyze numerical stability and computational overhead of our framework. Metrics: Condition number, numerical error, diagnostic overhead.
Experiment 4: Evaluate scalability on large-scale models and datasets. Metrics: Inference speed, memory usage, scalability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Curvature diagnostics may not fully capture precision requirements in all scenarios, potentially leading to suboptimal precision routing.
The computational overhead of curvature diagnostics could offset the benefits of precision reduction, especially in low-resource settings.
The framework assumes access to curvature information, which may not always be feasible in practical deployment scenarios.
